Oil is long considered an inelastic commodity as you can't really switch off demand.
It looks like China has managed to do this.

quoted_text:
Ken Griffin: oil should be near $200. It is near $100.

The reason is China, and even Goldman missed the size of it.

With wars in the Middle East and Europe, most estimates had crude near $200 if the strait closed. Griffin says China curtailed demand with far more elasticity than anyone forecast, and even Raj Mahajan, running a large Goldman commodity desk, admits they did not see the magnitude.

Louis Gave @gave_vincent has argued China's demand shifts drive the global commodity cycle more tha

quoted_text:
South Africa plans to increase its strategic oil reserves for the first time since the apartheid government stockpiled crude, adding to measures across the continent to mitigate supply shocks.

The country last built up its emergency stocks in the 1970s, when the United Nations imposed sanctions on the state due to its policy of institutionalized racial segregation, leading to the construction of the 45 million-barrel Saldanha Bay storage hub on the Atlantic coast
#oott https://t.co/6SDTraSbLA

text:
Indian-flagged LPG tankers have largely stayed out of the Persian Gulf. 

Only Nanda Devi remains in the Gulf of Oman. 

Four tankers are lifting LPG from the US, while two are berthed off Gujarat, reflecting a clear shift in trade routes.
